---
title: "Systematic Review Protocol"
subtitle: "Community Health Worker Interventions for Maternal Mental Health in Low- and Middle-Income Countries — PROSPERO Registration Draft"
author:
  - name: "Demo Researcher, PhD, MPH"
    affiliation:
      - name: "Example University School of Public Health"
        city: "Washington"
        region: "DC"
        country: "United States"
    email: "demo@example.org"
    corresponding: true
date: "2026-03-01"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    self-contained: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ../apa.csl
---

# Registration Information

- **Registration platform:** PROSPERO
- **Registration number:** CRD42026XXXXXX (pending)
- **Date of registration:** 2026-03-01

# Review Question

## Research Question

What is the effectiveness of community health worker (CHW) delivered mental health interventions on maternal depression and anxiety symptoms among pregnant and postpartum women in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs)?

## Question Framework

- **Framework used:** PICO
- **Population:** Pregnant and postpartum women (perinatal period, from conception through 12 months postpartum) in low- and middle-income countries as defined by the World Bank classification
- **Intervention:** Mental health interventions delivered by community health workers, lay health workers, peer supporters, or other non-specialist health workers. Includes psychoeducation, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)-based approaches, interpersonal therapy, problem-solving therapy, and psychosocial support
- **Comparator:** Standard care, enhanced usual care, waitlist control, or no intervention
- **Outcome:** Primary: Maternal depression symptoms measured by validated instruments (EPDS, PHQ-9, BDI, HDRS). Secondary: Anxiety symptoms, functional impairment, infant outcomes

# Background and Rationale

Perinatal depression affects an estimated 15–25% of women in low- and middle-income countries, substantially higher than the 10–15% prevalence in high-income settings [@fisher2012prevalence]. The treatment gap for mental health disorders in LMICs exceeds 90%, driven by severe shortages of mental health professionals [@lund2011poverty]. Task-shifting mental health interventions to community health workers has emerged as a promising strategy to bridge this gap [@singla2017psychological].

Several landmark trials have demonstrated the feasibility and effectiveness of CHW-delivered interventions. The Thinking Healthy Programme in Pakistan showed significant reductions in perinatal depression through CHW-delivered cognitive behavioral therapy [@rahman2008cognitive]. The Healthy Activity Program in India demonstrated similar benefits using lay counselors [@patel2017effectiveness]. However, the evidence base has grown considerably, and a comprehensive synthesis is needed to inform global maternal mental health policy.

This review is needed because: (1) the number of trials has increased substantially since the last comprehensive review; (2) intervention models have diversified; (3) LMICs vary widely in health system capacity; and (4) policy makers need pooled effect estimates to justify investment in task-shifting approaches.

# Objectives

1. To estimate the pooled effect of CHW-delivered mental health interventions on maternal depression symptoms in LMICs
2. To estimate the pooled effect on maternal anxiety symptoms
3. To explore heterogeneity by intervention type, delivery format, country income level, and depression measurement instrument
4. To assess the quality of evidence using the GRADE framework

# Eligibility Criteria

## Inclusion Criteria

| Criterion | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| Population | Pregnant or postpartum women (up to 12 months) in LMICs |
| Intervention | Mental health intervention delivered by CHW, lay health worker, peer supporter, or non-specialist |
| Comparator | Standard care, enhanced usual care, waitlist, or no intervention |
| Outcome | Depression and/or anxiety measured by validated instrument |
| Study design | Randomized controlled trials (individual or cluster), quasi-experimental studies with control group |
| Time period | Published January 2005 – March 2026 |
| Language | English |

## Exclusion Criteria

- Interventions delivered exclusively by mental health professionals (psychiatrists, psychologists, psychiatric nurses)
- Studies in high-income countries
- Conference abstracts without full-text publication
- Protocol papers without results
- Studies focusing solely on infant/child outcomes without maternal mental health measures
- Non-comparative designs (single-arm studies, case series)

# Information Sources

## Electronic Databases

| Database | Platform | Date searched |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| PubMed/MEDLINE | NCBI E-utilities (via RWA MCP) | 2026-03-02 |
| OpenAlex | REST API (via RWA MCP) | 2026-03-02 |
| Semantic Scholar | Academic Graph API (via RWA MCP) | 2026-03-02 |
| Europe PMC | REST API (via RWA MCP) | 2026-03-02 |
| CrossRef | REST API (via RWA MCP) | 2026-03-02 |

## Other Sources

- Reference lists of included studies and relevant systematic reviews
- WHO publications on maternal mental health and task-shifting
- Trial registries (ClinicalTrials.gov, ISRCTN)

# Search Strategy

## Boolean Query Structure

```
(Perinatal/maternal population terms)
AND (Depression/anxiety/mental health terms)
AND (CHW/lay worker/task-shifting terms)
AND (LMIC/developing country terms)
```

Full database-specific search strategies are documented in `search-strategy.qmd`.

# Study Selection

## Screening Process

1. **Deduplication:** Automated deduplication across databases, followed by manual review of suspected duplicates
2. **Title/abstract screening:** One reviewer screens all records, with 20% double-screened by a second reviewer for reliability
3. **Full-text screening:** Two reviewers independently assess all full-text articles; disagreements resolved by discussion
4. **Disagreement resolution:** Consensus discussion; third reviewer consulted if needed

# Data Extraction

## Data Items

| Variable | Description | Source |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Study ID | First author, year | Publication |
| Country | Country where study was conducted | Publication |
| Income level | World Bank classification at time of study | World Bank data |
| Sample size | N per arm (intervention, control) | Publication |
| Population | Pregnant vs. postpartum, gestational age or infant age | Publication |
| Intervention type | CBT, IPT, psychoeducation, psychosocial support, other | Publication |
| Delivery format | Individual, group, or mixed | Publication |
| CHW training | Duration and content of CHW training | Publication |
| Sessions | Number and frequency of sessions | Publication |
| Depression measure | Instrument used (EPDS, PHQ-9, BDI, HDRS) | Publication |
| Baseline depression | Mean and SD at baseline per arm | Publication |
| Endpoint depression | Mean and SD at endpoint per arm | Publication |
| Follow-up | Duration of follow-up in months | Publication |
| Effect size | Reported effect size and confidence interval | Publication or calculated |

# Risk of Bias Assessment

- **Tool:** RoB 2.0 for individual RCTs [@sterne2019rob2]
- **Domains assessed:** Randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of the outcome, selection of the reported result
- **Number of assessors:** Two independent assessors
- **Disagreement resolution:** Consensus discussion

# Data Synthesis

## Qualitative Synthesis

Narrative synthesis grouping studies by intervention type (CBT-based, IPT-based, psychoeducation, multicomponent) and describing patterns in effectiveness across settings and populations.

## Quantitative Synthesis

- **Software:** R 4.4+ with metafor package [@viechtbauer2010metafor]
- **Effect measure:** Standardized mean difference (SMD, Hedges' g) for continuous depression scores
- **Model:** Random-effects model with restricted maximum likelihood (REML) estimator
- **Heterogeneity assessment:** $I^2$ statistic, Cochran's $Q$ test, $\tau^2$ [@higgins2003measuring]
- **Subgroup analyses:**
  - Intervention type (CBT-based vs. other)
  - Country income level (low-income vs. lower-middle-income vs. upper-middle-income)
  - Depression measurement instrument (EPDS vs. PHQ-9 vs. other)
  - Delivery format (individual vs. group)
- **Sensitivity analyses:**
  - Excluding studies at high risk of bias
  - Excluding cluster-RCTs
  - Leave-one-out analysis
- **Publication bias:** Funnel plot visual inspection and Egger's regression test [@egger1997bias]

# Confidence in Cumulative Evidence

- **Framework:** GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development and Evaluations)
- **Domains assessed:** Risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, publication bias

# Use of Research Workflow Assistant (RWA)

This protocol was prepared with assistance from Research Workflow Assistant (RWA) [@vanzyl2026rwa], accessed through GitHub Copilot in VS Code:

- **Use:** Search strategy development and translation across database-specific syntaxes, protocol template generation, workflow planning
- **Human oversight:** All decisions regarding the research question, eligibility criteria, methods, analysis plan, and final approval were made by the research team
- **ICMJE compliance:** AI tools are not listed as authors; use is disclosed per ICMJE Section II.A.4

# Timeline

| Phase | Target Start | Target End |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| Protocol registration | March 2026 | March 2026 |
| Search execution | March 2026 | March 2026 |
| Screening | March 2026 | April 2026 |
| Data extraction | April 2026 | May 2026 |
| Risk of bias assessment | May 2026 | May 2026 |
| Data synthesis | June 2026 | July 2026 |
| Manuscript preparation | July 2026 | August 2026 |
| Submission | September 2026 | September 2026 |

# Amendments

Any amendments to this protocol will be documented with date, description, and rationale.

| Date | Section | Amendment | Rationale |
|------|---------|-----------|-----------|
| — | — | No amendments to date | — |

# References
